---
title: "Warm-up: sequence length"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

First contact with [`&[u8]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html) (a borrowed view of bytes) — write the one-line function that returns its length. This is the smallest possible Rust function over a sequence, and a gentle way to check that your toolchain (Rust compiler + `cargo`) is wired up correctly before tackling [GC content](01-gc-content.qmd).

## Background

In Rust, the length of a slice is a [`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.usize.html) (Rust's unsigned integer type for lengths and indices, sized to fit any address on your machine). Every slice supports the [`seq.len()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.len) method (the standard slice-length method), which returns that `usize` in constant time — the slice already knows how long it is.

That is the entire problem: take a `&[u8]`, return its `len()`.

## The exercise

Open the starter project:

```bash
cd day1/ex-sequence-length
```

You will see a single Rust source file at `src/main.rs`. It already contains:

- A function `fn length(seq: &[u8]) -> usize` with a `// TODO` body
- A `main` function that reads a string from the command line and calls `length`
- A `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block with three unit tests (empty input, `ACGT`, and a longer string)

Your job: replace the `TODO` body so that all three tests pass.

## How to run

From inside `day1/ex-sequence-length/`:

```bash
cargo test            # run the tests — these should be failing at first
cargo run -- ACGT     # run the program and see the length printed
```

When the function is correct, `cargo test` will print:

```
test result: ok. 3 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

and your program should print:

```
$ cargo run -- ACGT
length: 4
```

## Hint

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — One method call"}
Every slice in Rust has a [`len()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.len) method that returns its length as a [`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.usize.html). Just call it on `seq` and return the result.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn length(seq: &[u8]) -> usize {
    seq.len()
}
```

The full reference implementation lives in `day1/ex-sequence-length-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

The next warm-up looks at the *first* and *last* base of a slice — same shape of function, but now you have to think about what to return when the slice is empty.
